headlines.geopolitics: Navy veteran with no family buried with full military honors as community refuses to let him be buried alone
headlines.economy: DOJ launches new fraud division targeting 'insane' government benefit fraud
headlines.domestic: Fox poll shows 87% expect AI to transform lives, but only 47% find it useful today

metadata.daily_do: Check if there are unclaimed veterans in your area who need community support for their final honors

clips.caption: When Duke gets unexpectedly emotional about veterans
Pringle explains why ancient Greeks would understand our AI fears
Jax's take on AI weapons finding office chairs

script.text: Look, I don't care what your politics are — when a man serves his country and dies alone, that's when you find out what kind of people we actually are.
Good morning, I'm Leo Barros and this is The Friction. It's Monday, March 10th, and we're starting with three stories that actually matter.

First up — geopolitics isn't always about missiles and trade wars. Sometimes it's about who we are as people. In Tennessee last week, a Navy veteran named Lonnie Wayman died with no known family to claim his remains.
Oh no.

The VA labeled him 'unclaimed.' But when word got out, hundreds of veterans and community members showed up at Middle Tennessee State Veterans Cemetery to give him full military honors. The VA rep said something I want to quote directly — 'Thanks to all the support I see here today, we are able to claim our honorable veterans and provide them the dignity and honor they have earned.'
Damn right.
That's— sorry, that actually got me.

Economy — Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche announced the DOJ is creating an entirely new fraud division after what he calls 'insane' levels of government benefit fraud. He cited cases like two Pennsylvania men who got four million dollars in federal funds for fake homeless shelters in Minneapolis.
Four million?
That's what happens when you give money away like candy.
Blanche says they're going after fraud of any size — quote, 'no fraud too small, no fraud too large.' If you steal a thousand and one dollars, that's a felony, and you're going to prison.
Very specific number there.

And domestic — a new Fox News poll shows eighty-seven percent of voters expect AI to significantly change American life. But here's the disconnect — only forty-seven percent find AI useful in their daily lives right now.
That tracks. We're all waiting for the robot revolution but my phone still can't figure out what I'm saying half the time.
Eighty percent are concerned about AI eroding trust in news and social media. And here's a bonus finding — seventy-four percent of Americans believe life exists on other planets.
Honestly, after this week's news cycle, I'm ready to apply for citizenship elsewhere.
The aliens are probably reading our polls and deciding to keep their distance.
Alright, let's dig into what happened in Tennessee, because I think this story says something important about who we are right now.

So Lonnie Wayman, Navy veteran, dies in Tennessee. No known family. The paperwork says 'unclaimed veteran.' But word gets out, and hundreds of people show up to his funeral. Veterans, community members, strangers. Duke, you served — what does this mean to you?

Look, I don't care what your politics are — when a man serves his country and dies alone, that's when you find out what kind of people we actually are. And Tennessee showed up. That's America at its best.
But it's also America at its worst, right? Like, where were we when this man was alive? How does someone who served our country end up with no support system, no family, no—
Whoa, whoa. You don't know his story. Maybe he chose to be alone. Maybe he had family drama. Maybe he pushed people away. We can't fix everyone's life.
I'm not saying we can, but— look, we have a massive veteran homelessness problem, suicide rates through the roof, and then we act surprised when someone dies alone?
Hold on. The story here isn't the failure. The story is that when it mattered, people showed up. They didn't know this man, but they knew he served, and that was enough.

Can I just say something? My grandfather was a Korean War vet, and he used to say the guys who came back the most messed up were the ones who came back to nothing. No parade, no recognition, just— back to regular life.
That's exactly right. And that's why this matters. These people in Tennessee, they gave him what every veteran deserves — recognition that his service mattered.
I'm not disagreeing with that, I'm saying— okay, yes, beautiful moment, really moving. But can we also acknowledge that we have systems that are failing veterans while they're alive?
Of course the systems are failing. The VA is a disaster. But you know what worked? Community. People caring about people. Not government programs, not bureaucrats — neighbors.
Neighbors are great, but neighbors can't provide healthcare, or housing assistance, or—
Actually, in Tennessee, neighbors just provided a military funeral with full honors. So maybe neighbors can do more than you think.

Here's what I keep coming back to — the VA rep said the paperwork was wrong. That 'unclaimed' was a misnomer. Because the community claimed him. That's not just nice words, that's a different way of thinking about what we owe each other.
If I may — this reminds me of something quite profound from antiquity.
Oh here we go.
No, I actually want to hear this. Doc?
Professor, take us to school.

Well, this Tennessee funeral is not unprecedented. In fact, it's part of a tradition that goes back to the very foundation of organized society. The Romans had a concept called 'honor debitum' — honor owed.

But the more direct parallel is the Tomb of the Unknown Soldier. We built that monument in 1921, after the Great War, because we realized that some service transcends individual identity. The unknown soldier represents every soldier.
Right, exactly.

Now, here's what's fascinating about Tennessee. In 1921, we had to create a formal monument to honor anonymous service. But in 2026, a community spontaneously created their own version. No government mandate, no congressional appropriation. Just people understanding what honor requires.
That's actually beautiful.

The Romans would have understood this perfectly. They believed that the community itself could be dishonored by failing to properly honor those who served it. Not the government — the community. The people themselves bore that responsibility.
So basically, if they hadn't shown up, they would have dishonored themselves?
Precisely. The Romans had a phrase — 'civitas sine honore' — a city without honor. They believed that how you treat your dead, especially your war dead, reveals the character of the living.
And Tennessee passed the test.

Tennessee passed the test. But here's the deeper lesson — this wasn't about government policy or veteran affairs budgets. This was about something much older and more fundamental. The understanding that service creates a debt that survives death.
Professor, thank you. That actually reframes the whole story for me.
Me too. And I think— look, I still think we need better systems for living veterans. But this shows that the values are still there. People still understand what matters.
Sometimes the people get it right even when the government gets it wrong.
Alright, I need to lighten this up before I start crying at work. Leo, can we move to the part where the government is stealing money?
Actually, Jax, it's the reverse. People are stealing money from the government. But yes, let's talk about the DOJ's new fraud division.

So Deputy AG Todd Blanche says fraud is 'insane' and they're creating a whole new division to go after it. His example — two guys from Pennsylvania drove to Minneapolis, signed up to run homeless shelters that didn't exist, and walked away with four million dollars.
This is exactly what I've been saying for years. The government gives money away like candy, nobody checks anything, and then we act shocked when people game the system.
Okay, but here's my question — how do you crack down on fraud without making it impossible for people who actually need help to get it?
You verify. You audit. You don't just send checks to anyone who asks nicely.
Right, but verification takes time and money and bureaucracy, and meanwhile people are hungry or homeless or—
Um, can I just point out that the fake homeless shelter guys probably made it harder for actual homeless people to get help? Like, that four million could have actually housed people?
That's— yeah, that's actually a really good point.
Thank you. Every dollar stolen from a fake program is a dollar not helping real people. Fraud isn't a victimless crime.

Blanche also mentioned school lunch fraud and daycare centers that don't exist. He says the feds get bills like 'send us two point nine million this month for lunches' and they just send it without checking.
Wait, school lunch fraud? That's— okay, that's actually infuriating. Those programs feed kids who might not eat otherwise.
Now you're getting it. This isn't about being mean to poor people. This is about making sure the money actually reaches poor people.
Can we talk about the thousand-and-one-dollar thing? Like, why is that the magic number? Did some lawyer just really like that extra dollar?
That's the federal felony threshold. A thousand or less is usually a misdemeanor. A thousand and one makes it a federal case with serious prison time.
And that's the point. Blanche is saying we're going to prosecute every case, no matter how small. Make it not worth the risk.
I mean, I get the deterrent effect, but are we really going to send someone to prison for stealing a thousand dollars because they couldn't pay rent?
If they steal it from a program meant to help other people who can't pay rent? Yeah, absolutely.
Alright, let's move to our last story before Professor Pringle tells us about ancient Roman attitudes toward artificial intelligence.
Actually, the Greeks were quite worried about mechanical servants. Hephaestus had golden automata, and Plato warned about—
Oh my God, he actually has a take on this.
Professor, hold that thought. Let's set up the AI story first.

So this Fox News poll shows a fascinating disconnect. Eighty-seven percent of Americans expect AI to change our lives significantly. But only forty-seven percent find it useful right now. It's like we're all waiting for the robot revolution while Siri still can't understand what we're saying.
This tracks with every new technology, right? Like, everyone knew the internet was going to be huge in 1995, but most people were still using dial-up and thought email was confusing.
Yeah, but the difference is the internet didn't threaten to replace human judgment. AI is supposed to think for us, and that's— that's different.
I asked ChatGPT to write my grocery list last week, and it suggested I buy quinoa. I don't even know what quinoa is. The AI revolution is trying to make me eat healthier and I'm not having it.
But that's actually the point, right? The gap between what AI can theoretically do and what it actually does for regular people is huge.
The poll also found eighty percent are concerned about AI eroding trust in news and social media. Which is ironic, since trust in news and social media was already pretty eroded.
That's the real problem. We can't tell what's real anymore. Photos, videos, articles — could all be fake. How do you have a democracy when nobody knows what's true?
And eighty-nine percent want clear disclosure when content is AI-generated. But who's going to enforce that? How do you even verify it?
I love that we're worried about AI taking over while also admitting we can't figure out how to make it useful. It's like being afraid of a robot uprising while your Roomba is stuck under the couch.
Professor, you mentioned the Greeks had thoughts about artificial intelligence?

Oh indeed. The Greeks were quite sophisticated about this question. Hephaestus, the god of craftsmanship, created golden automata — mechanical servants. But Plato warned about the danger of replacing human wisdom with mechanical calculation.

In the Republic, Plato argues that the highest form of knowledge is wisdom — sophia — which requires judgment, experience, and moral reasoning. He feared that if people became dependent on mechanical aids for thinking, they would atrophy their capacity for genuine understanding.
So basically, use it or lose it.

Precisely. But here's what's fascinating about your poll results — the Greeks would have understood perfectly why people expect transformation but don't find current AI useful. They distinguished between techne — technical skill — and phronesis — practical wisdom.

Current AI is very good at techne — pattern recognition, data processing, optimization. But it has no phronesis — no understanding of context, values, human needs. That's why it can write poetry but suggests quinoa to Jax.
I can't believe ancient Greeks are taking shots at my diet.
But that's actually really helpful. Like, maybe the problem isn't that AI isn't smart enough — maybe we're asking it to do the wrong things?
The Greeks would say yes, absolutely. Use mechanical aids for mechanical tasks. But preserve human judgment for questions that require wisdom, ethics, and understanding of the good life.
Which brings us to the other poll finding — seventy-four percent of Americans believe life exists on other planets. Any ancient wisdom on that, Professor?
Well, Democritus believed in infinite worlds with infinite possibilities. Though I suspect he didn't anticipate they'd be avoiding us after reading our polling data.
Alright, speaking of infinite possibilities, let's get weird with Rapid Fire. I've got some stories that prove reality is stranger than any AI could generate.

First up — Palantir, the company that makes AI for the military, just deployed their weapons targeting system to help government workers find office chairs. I'm not making this up. The same AI that tracks terrorists is now optimizing seating arrangements.
That's actually the most government thing I've ever heard.
Wait, is this a joke about military-industrial complex bureaucracy, or did this actually happen?
Both! The future is we're going to get killed by a robot that's really, really good at ergonomics.
I'm genuinely not sure if that makes me feel better or worse about AI.

Next — a boss in Ireland repeatedly shouted 'potato' at his Irish employee as workplace harassment. The employee won the case. Apparently, ethnic slurs involving root vegetables are still slurs. Who knew?
The fact that had to go to court is the real story.
The Irish potato famine killed over a million people. Using that as mockery is particularly cruel.
Thank you, Professor. And thank you for making that somehow more depressing.
Louisiana schools have until Friday to post the Ten Commandments in every classroom. Because apparently separation of church and state is more of a guideline than a rule now.
That's going to get overturned so fast it'll break the speed of light.
Maybe, but Louisiana's gonna try it anyway. States are pushing back on federal overreach however they can.

And finally, a Florida drug dealer locked a man in a storage unit for months over an unpaid debt. The man survived by eating ramen and drinking rainwater. Florida Man strikes again, but this time he's the victim.
That's not funny, Jax. That's kidnapping and torture.
You're right, you're right. I just— the Florida of it all makes my brain default to comedy when it should default to horror.
That guy's going to need serious trauma therapy. Like, that's months of psychological torture.
And the dealer's going to prison for a very long time. Good.
Alright, that's Rapid Fire. And that's why I stick to coffee debt instead of drug debt. Much safer for everyone involved.
Time for the Daily Do. Today's story about Lonnie Wayman got me thinking — there are probably unclaimed veterans in your area right now.

Here's what you can do: contact your local VA office or veterans cemetery and ask if they have any upcoming services for unclaimed veterans. Many places maintain lists and would welcome community support.
You don't have to organize anything. Just show up. Stand quietly. Honor someone who served your country. It takes an hour, and it means everything.
And if you're a veteran yourself, reach out to other vets. Check on people. We're better together than alone.
There are also organizations like Missing in America Project that help identify and honor unclaimed veterans. Easy to find online.
That's your Daily Do. Professor, send us home with some wisdom.
Marcus Aurelius wrote, 'Death smiles at us all, but all a man can do is smile back.' The people of Tennessee smiled back for Lonnie Wayman. That's how civilizations endure.
I'm Jax Mooney. We'll be back tomorrow with more news that matters and probably more ancient wisdom about modern problems. Thanks for listening to The Friction.
